report_metadata.stage: Kadish C (intracranial extension), Hyams Grade III
molecular_profile: Potential IDH2 R172 mutation (35% in basal-like subtype)

treatments.name: Craniofacial Resection + Adjuvant Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT)

evidence_level.rationale: Decades of institutional series and meta-analyses consistently demonstrate superior outcomes with surgery plus adjuvant RT. Largest series (931 patients) showed HR 0.22 for combined modality. 5-year OS 84.4% for surgery+RT in 64-patient series. Not phase 3 RCT due to rarity, but highest available evidence for ENB.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 70.8-84.4% for Kadish C with combined modality therapy. 5-year local control 85.9% with adjuvant RT vs 72.7% without. Hyams III 5-year OS approximately 73.7%. Represents best achievable survival for this stage/grade.

accessibility.rationale: Available at all major academic medical centers with skull base surgery programs. IMRT widely available. Established standard of care approach requiring no special drug access.

safety_profile.rationale: Craniofacial resection carries risks of CSF leak (1-6%), meningitis (1.8-4.5%), and anosmia. IMRT late toxicities infrequent (11.5%) including dysosmia, hearing loss, rare temporal lobe necrosis. Endoscopic approach reduces morbidity significantly.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability regardless of molecular profile. Standard approach for all ENB patients with Kadish C disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical resection of tumor via craniofacial approach (open or endoscopic endonasal) removes gross disease including intracranial extension. Adjuvant IMRT (60-70 Gy) delivers conformal radiation to the tumor bed and margins to eliminate microscopic residual disease while sparing critical structures (optic nerves, brainstem, temporal lobes).

key_evidence.study_name: Long-Term Survival Outcomes and Treatment Experience of 64 Patients With Esthesioneuroblastoma

treatments.availability: Widely available at academic medical centers

treatments.name: Endoscopic Endonasal Surgery (Minimally Invasive Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple systematic reviews and large institutional series compare endoscopic vs open approaches. 2025 Frontiers systematic review and 2022 World Journal of Otorhinolaryngology meta-analysis confirm equivalent or superior outcomes. 5-year PFS 61.7% (endoscopic) vs 22.2% (open).

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 76.2-79.3% for endoscopic approach in Kadish C. Disease-free survival significantly higher vs open surgery. Lower complication rates support better functional outcomes. Must achieve negative margins; not suitable for all Kadish C presentations.

accessibility.rationale: Requires surgeon experienced in endoscopic skull base surgery. Available at major academic centers with trained rhinology/neurosurgery teams. International consensus statement (2019) provides guidance on appropriate case selection for Kadish C.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly lower complication rates than open craniofacial resection: CSF leak 1.0% vs 6.0%, meningitis 1.8% vs 4.5%. Shorter hospitalization. Faster recovery. No external incision. Lower blood loss.

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all ENB regardless of molecular profile, provided negative margins can be achieved. Suitable for Kadish C without lateral orbital, palate, or midface extension per 2019 consensus.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Purely endoscopic endonasal approach accesses the tumor through the nasal cavity using endoscopes and specialized instruments. Achieves complete tumor resection including crista galli, cribriform plate, and dura as needed, with nasoseptal flap reconstruction of the skull base defect. Avoids bifrontal craniotomy and facial incisions of traditional craniofacial resection.

key_evidence.study_name: Endoscopic surgery versus various open approaches in esthesioneuroblastoma: a systematic review

treatments.availability: Available at major academic skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Cisplatin/Etoposide + Surgery + Adjuvant RT

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple institutional series support neoadjuvant platinum-etoposide for Kadish C ENB. Objective response rate 82% (95% CI 52-95%) in prospective series. University of Virginia protocol well-established. No randomized trials but consistent retrospective data.

survival_benefit.rationale: University of Virginia protocol: 5-year OS 72%, 10-year OS 60% for advanced disease. Neoadjuvant chemo may downstage tumors for more complete resection. Mayo Clinic data showed benefit in high-grade stage C (median time to relapse 35 vs 10.5 months with adjuvant chemo).

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and etoposide are generic, widely available chemotherapeutics. Requires oncology center experienced with neuroendocrine tumor management. Protocol well-known at major centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin/etoposide causes significant hematologic toxicity with grade 3/4 neutropenia in 37% of cycles. Additional risks include nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting, and neuropathy. Combined with surgical and RT toxicity adds cumulative burden.

biomarker_match.rationale: ENB has neuroendocrine differentiation making it responsive to platinum-etoposide regimens similar to other neuroendocrine tumors. Synaptophysin and chromogranin positivity confirm neuroendocrine phenotype suitable for this approach.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant cisplatin (75 mg/m2 day 1) and etoposide (100 mg/m2 days 1-3) given for 2-3 cycles every 21 days to reduce tumor burden before definitive surgery. Cisplatin forms DNA cross-links causing apoptosis; etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II. Tumor shrinkage facilitates more complete surgical resection, followed by adjuvant radiation (50-66 Gy) to consolidate local control.

key_evidence.study_name: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced olfactory neuroblastoma with intracranial extension

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant or Definitive)

evidence_level.rationale: Single-institution series of 14 patients showed 2-year locoregional control 88.9%, 2-year OS 84.4%. Systematic reviews confirm charged particle therapy associated with improved OS vs photon irradiation for sinonasal malignancies. No randomized comparison but dosimetric advantages well-established.

survival_benefit.rationale: 2-year OS 84.4%, 2-year PFS 75.0% in proton therapy series. Allows dose escalation to 63-70 GyRBE while sparing critical structures. Higher local control rates translate to survival benefit, especially for Kadish C with intracranial extension near brainstem and optic apparatus.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers are limited globally. Available at approximately 40+ centers in the US and growing number worldwide, but still not universally accessible. Requires referral to specialized center. Insurance coverage increasingly accepted for skull base tumors.

safety_profile.rationale: Superior dose conformality reduces radiation to adjacent critical structures. No treatment interruptions in clinical series. Late toxicities limited to cataract (14%) and glaucoma (7%). Lower risk of temporal lobe necrosis and cognitive decline compared to photon IMRT.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability for all ENB regardless of molecular profile. Particularly advantageous for Kadish C tumors with intracranial extension given proximity to critical neural structures.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beam therapy exploits the Bragg peak phenomenon where protons deposit maximum energy at a defined depth with minimal exit dose. This allows delivery of tumoricidal doses (63-70 GyRBE) to the skull base tumor bed while dramatically reducing radiation exposure to adjacent optic nerves, chiasm, brainstem, temporal lobes, and hippocampi compared to photon-based IMRT.

key_evidence.study_name: Upright proton therapy for esthesioneuroblastoma: a single-institution experience

treatments.availability: Available at specialized proton therapy centers (40+ US centers)

treatments.name: Cyclophosphamide/Vincristine/Doxorubicin (CVD) Neoadjuvant Regimen

evidence_level.rationale: University of Virginia protocol established over decades with published long-term outcomes. 5-year OS 72%, 10-year OS 60% for Kadish C patients. Mishima protocol (CVD+cisplatin/etoposide) achieved 67% complete response. Retrospective but consistent institutional data.

survival_benefit.rationale: UVA protocol: 5-year OS 72%, 10-year OS 60% for advanced disease. Mishima intensive protocol: 8/12 patients achieved complete response. Enables tumor downstaging for improved surgical outcomes. Some patients become resectable after initially unresectable disease.

accessibility.rationale: All agents (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin) are generic and widely available. Well-established dosing protocols. Can be administered at any oncology center with chemotherapy infusion capability.

safety_profile.rationale: Multi-agent chemotherapy carries significant toxicity burden. Doxorubicin causes cumulative cardiotoxicity (lifetime dose limit). Vincristine causes peripheral neuropathy. Cyclophosphamide causes hemorrhagic cystitis. Grade 3/4 myelosuppression common. May require G-CSF support.

biomarker_match.rationale: Neuroendocrine tumors responsive to this combination. Well-suited for synaptophysin+/chromogranin+ ENB. No specific biomarker selection needed beyond neuroendocrine differentiation confirmation.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Three-drug neoadjuvant regimen: cyclophosphamide (300-650 mg/m2) alkylates DNA causing cross-links; vincristine (1-2 mg) disrupts microtubules arresting mitosis; doxorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II. Given for 2 cycles prior to radiation (50 Gy) followed by craniofacial resection per the University of Virginia protocol.

key_evidence.study_name: The role of preoperative adjuvant treatment in the management of esthesioneuroblastoma: the University of Virginia experience

treatments.name: Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS/Gamma Knife/CyberKnife)

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter study of 31 tumors in 13 patients showed 89% local control (48% reduction + 41% stable). Stanford CyberKnife series treated 32 brain metastases in 8 patients. No radiation-related complications. Multiple small institutional series support efficacy.

survival_benefit.rationale: 89% local control rate for recurrent disease. 5-year OS 63% and PFS 56% after salvage therapy (including SRS). Particularly valuable for brain metastases and locally recurrent disease in previously irradiated field. Non-invasive alternative to repeat surgery.

accessibility.rationale: Gamma Knife and CyberKnife platforms available at many academic centers and some community hospitals. Growing number of installations worldwide. Requires specialized neurosurgery/radiation oncology team for skull base targets.

safety_profile.rationale: No radiation-related symptomatic complications in multicenter study. Non-invasive outpatient procedure. Single-fraction or hypofractionated delivery. Minimal acute toxicity. Low risk of radiation necrosis with appropriate dose constraints.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability regardless of molecular profile. Effective for all ENB subtypes in recurrent or metastatic setting.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Highly focused, single-fraction or hypofractionated radiation delivery using multiple convergent beams (Gamma Knife) or robotic linear accelerator (CyberKnife) to deliver ablative radiation doses to well-defined tumor targets while sparing surrounding normal tissue. Particularly suited for recurrent disease in previously irradiated fields or brain metastases where conventional re-irradiation is limited.

key_evidence.study_name: Radiosurgery is an Effective Treatment for Recurrent Esthesioneuroblastoma: A Multicenter Study
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

treatments.availability: Available at Gamma Knife and CyberKnife centers (200+ US centers)

treatments.name: Concurrent Cisplatin-Based Chemoradiation

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective analysis of 931 patients showed cisplatin-based concurrent chemoradiation after surgery improved outcomes (HR 0.22, p<0.01) vs RT alone. Limited prospective data. Some conflicting results in smaller series showing no significant survival difference vs surgery alone.

survival_benefit.rationale: HR 0.22 in large retrospective analysis suggests substantial benefit when combined with surgery. 5-year disease-free survival 68% for surgical approach vs 42% for CCRT alone, but CCRT patients typically had more advanced disease. Benefits most clear for high-grade, advanced-stage tumors.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin is generic and universally available. Concurrent chemoradiation protocols are standard across oncology centers. No specialized equipment beyond standard linear accelerator required.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicity of concurrent cisplatin with radiation is significant: mucositis, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, myelosuppression, nausea. Higher acute toxicity than sequential approach. Requires close monitoring and supportive care during treatment.

biomarker_match.rationale: Neuroendocrine tumors including ENB show platinum sensitivity. Synaptophysin and chromogranin positivity support neuroendocrine phenotype responsive to platinum agents. No specific biomarker selection required.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Concurrent administration of cisplatin (typically 60-100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks or 40 mg/m2 weekly) with radiation therapy (60-70 Gy). Cisplatin acts as a radiosensitizer by inhibiting DNA repair and enhancing radiation-induced cell death. The synergistic effect targets both local tumor and micrometastatic disease simultaneously.

key_evidence.study_name: Retrospective analysis of cisplatin-based CT-RT vs RT alone in esthesioneuroblastoma

treatments.availability: Widely available at all oncology centers

treatments.name: Elective Neck Irradiation / Neck Dissection for Nodal Management

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple retrospective studies address elective neck management. ENI associated with improved 5-year regional control (100% vs 82%). 2024 propensity-score matched study supports ENI. Cervical metastases eventually occur in 20-25% without elective treatment.

survival_benefit.rationale: ENI improves regional control (100% vs 82% at 5 years) but has not shown statistically significant OS or DFS improvement. Prevents cervical nodal recurrence which occurs in 20-25% of patients. Salvage neck dissection achieves 87% 2-year control for those who recur.

accessibility.rationale: Standard radiation oncology and surgical technique available at all cancer centers. No specialized equipment beyond standard linear accelerator. Neck dissection is routine head and neck surgical procedure.

safety_profile.rationale: ENI adds modest radiation dose to neck with mild additional dysphagia and skin reaction. Neck dissection risks include accessory nerve injury, lymphedema, wound complications. Both well-tolerated when properly planned.

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all ENB patients regardless of molecular profile. Particularly important for Hyams III/IV tumors which have higher rates of cervical nodal metastasis.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Elective nodal irradiation delivers prophylactic radiation (45-50 Gy) to the bilateral cervical lymph node levels I-III to eliminate occult micrometastatic disease. Neck dissection surgically removes lymph nodes at risk. Both approaches address the 20-25% incidence of eventual cervical nodal metastasis in ENB, particularly important for high-grade tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: The value of elective neck irradiation in management of esthesioneuroblastoma: a retrospective study based on propensity score matching

treatments.availability: Widely available at all cancer centers

treatments.name: 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Case series of 7 patients showed 57% partial response, 29% stable disease. Additional case reports confirm activity. FDA-approved for GEP-NETs (NETTER-1 trial) providing regulatory precedent. 2025 ASCO abstract reports outcomes in ONB case series. No ENB-specific randomized trial.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 17 months and median OS 32 months in 7-patient series of Kadish D (metastatic) disease. Case report showed 12-month disease control. Primarily studied in recurrent/metastatic setting where prognosis is otherwise poor.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved as Lutathera for GEP-NETs. Off-label use for ENB requires SSTR2-positive tumors confirmed by Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT. Available at specialized nuclear medicine centers. Insurance coverage variable for off-label indication.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated in ENB patients. Only grade 1 adverse events (fatigue, mild nausea, transient cytopenias) in case reports. Amino acid renal protection protocol minimizes nephrotoxicity. Renal function remained stable. Grade 3/4 neutropenia possible but uncommon.

biomarker_match.rationale: SSTR2 expression found in 82-99% of ONB cases. Mean H-score 189. Expression maintained in recurrences and metastases. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET confirms target expression before treatment. Highly relevant for this synaptophysin+/chromogranin+ neuroendocrine tumor.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: 177Lu-DOTATATE is a radiolabeled somatostatin analog that binds to somatostatin receptor type 2 (SSTR2), which is expressed in 82-99% of olfactory neuroblastomas. Upon binding, the complex is internalized, delivering targeted beta radiation (177-Lutetium) directly to tumor cells while sparing normal tissue. Administered IV at 7.4 GBq every 8 weeks for 4 cycles with amino acid renal protection.

key_evidence.study_name: Efficacy of Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy for Esthesioneuroblastoma

notable_side_effects: Potential nephrotoxicity (mitigated by amino acid infusion)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for GEP-NETs; off-label for ENB at specialized nuclear medicine centers

treatments.name: Carbon Ion Radiotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Limited data specific to ENB. Systematic reviews of skull base tumors show superiority over photon RT. 48-month OS 100% and PFS 81% for combined IMRT/carbon ion in previously unirradiated patients. Higher relative biological effectiveness (RBE ~3) than protons (RBE 1.1) but fewer clinical data.

survival_benefit.rationale: 48-month OS 100% and PFS 81% in mixed skull base tumor cohort including ENB. Higher RBE (~3x protons) may provide superior tumor kill for radiation-resistant, high-grade tumors. Theoretical advantage for Hyams III tumors that may be more radioresistant.

accessibility.rationale: Extremely limited availability: approximately 13 operating carbon ion facilities worldwide. None in most countries. Requires travel to Japan, Germany, Italy, Austria, or China. Very high treatment cost. Not covered by most insurance plans.

safety_profile.rationale: Similar to proton therapy in dose conformality but higher biological potency raises concern for late toxicity to critical structures. Limited long-term follow-up data for skull base ENB. Early results suggest acceptable toxicity profile.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability regardless of molecular profile. May be particularly advantageous for high-grade (Hyams III/IV) tumors that may show relative radioresistance to photon and proton therapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carbon ions have a higher linear energy transfer (LET) than protons, producing more dense ionization and greater biological damage per unit dose (RBE ~3 vs 1.1 for protons). This creates more complex, irreparable DNA damage including clustered double-strand breaks. The higher RBE may overcome radioresistance in high-grade neuroendocrine tumors. Carbon ions also exhibit a sharper Bragg peak for superior dose conformality.

key_evidence.study_name: The role of particle radiotherapy in the treatment of skull base tumors

treatments.name: Sunitinib (Multi-Target Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Limited to case reports and small series. One case showed disease stabilization for 15-24 months. Another showed 65% tumor reduction when combined with cetuximab. No prospective trials. Evidence based on molecular rationale (VEGFR, PDGFR, c-KIT, RET inhibition) and case-level data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Disease stabilization achieved for 15-24 months in metastatic/refractory setting. Karnofsky performance status improved from 40% to 70% in one case. Combined with cetuximab, 65% tumor reduction in 5 days. Potential benefit primarily in treatment-refractory disease.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for renal cell carcinoma and GIST. Available as oral medication. Off-label use for ENB requires molecular justification. Insurance coverage may require prior authorization. Widely stocked at oncology pharmacies.

safety_profile.rationale: Known TKI toxicity profile: hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, hypothyroidism, myelosuppression. Manageable with dose modifications. Generally well tolerated compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: ENB expresses VEGFR, PDGFR, and c-KIT which are sunitinib targets. Whole exome sequencing may identify additional targetable mutations (KDR, RET). Best candidates are those with confirmed target expression on molecular profiling.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Oral multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor that blocks VEGFR-1/2/3, PDGFR-alpha/beta, c-KIT, RET, and FLT3. Inhibits tumor angiogenesis by blocking VEGF signaling and directly inhibits tumor cell proliferation through PDGFR and c-KIT pathways. Disrupts the vascular supply critical for skull base tumor growth.

key_evidence.study_name: Disease stabilization of progressive olfactory neuroblastoma under treatment with sunitinib mesylate

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for other indications; off-label for ENB

treatments.name: Pazopanib (Multi-Target TKI)

evidence_level.rationale: Single case report of metastatic ENB with fumarate hydratase mutation showing durable partial response exceeding 4 years on pazopanib. Exceptional responder case. No prospective trials or larger series in ENB.

survival_benefit.rationale: Durable partial response exceeding 48 months in heavily pretreated metastatic ENB. Significant tumor reduction documented. Exceptional case but demonstrates proof-of-concept for TKI-responsive ENB, particularly with specific molecular alterations.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for renal cell carcinoma and soft tissue sarcoma. Oral medication. Off-label for ENB requires molecular profiling justification, ideally with identified targetable mutations. Available at oncology pharmacies.

safety_profile.rationale: Similar TKI toxicity profile to sunitinib: hepatotoxicity (requires LFT monitoring), hypertension, diarrhea, hair color changes, fatigue. Hepatotoxicity risk distinguishes it from other TKIs. Generally manageable with monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets VEGFR-1/2/3, PDGFR-alpha/beta, c-KIT. Best response documented in patient with fumarate hydratase mutation. Molecular profiling recommended to identify patients most likely to benefit. Not all ENB will respond.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR-1/2/3, PDGFR-alpha/beta, FGFR-1/3, and c-KIT. Inhibits angiogenesis and tumor cell proliferation. In the documented ENB responder, a pathogenic fumarate hydratase mutation may have created metabolic vulnerability enhancing sensitivity to angiogenesis inhibition through HIF-1alpha pathway dysregulation.

key_evidence.study_name: Durable Major Response With Pazopanib in Recurrent, Heavily Pretreated Metastatic Esthesioneuroblastoma Harboring a Fumarate Hydratase Mutation

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for other indications; off-label for ENB

treatments.name: Sorafenib (Multi-Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Single case report of dramatic response in chemotherapy-refractory ENB with extensive cranial invasion. Additional case of favorable outcome when combined with everolimus failure. Proof-of-concept but very limited evidence base.

survival_benefit.rationale: Dramatic tumor response documented in refractory ENB case. Represents potential salvage option when conventional therapies have failed. Limited data on duration of response and long-term outcomes.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for hepatocellular carcinoma, renal cell carcinoma, and differentiated thyroid cancer. Oral medication widely available. Off-label use for ENB on compassionate basis.

safety_profile.rationale: Hand-foot skin reaction (common), diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, rash. Cardiac toxicity risk. Known hepatotoxicity. Manageable side effect profile for oral targeted therapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets RAF kinase, VEGFR-2/3, PDGFR-beta, c-KIT, FLT3, RET. ENB expresses several of these targets. Molecular profiling may help identify optimal candidates. RAF/RAS pathway alterations found in subset of ENB.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting RAF/MEK/ERK signaling pathway, VEGFR-2/3, PDGFR-beta, c-KIT, FLT3, and RET. Dual mechanism inhibits both tumor cell proliferation (RAF pathway) and tumor angiogenesis (VEGFR). The RAF pathway inhibition distinguishes it from other TKIs and may be relevant given RAS pathway mutations identified in subset of ENB.

key_evidence.study_name: Dramatic response to targeted therapy in an aggressive olfactory neuroblastoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for other indications; off-label for ENB

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab/Nivolumab (PD-1 Checkpoint Inhibitors)

evidence_level.rationale: Very limited data in ENB specifically. PD-L1 expression conflicting (0-40% across studies). Case reports suggest potential activity in refractory disease. No prospective trials in ENB. Extrapolation from head and neck squamous cell carcinoma data is uncertain.

survival_benefit.rationale: Promise in refractory cases per case reports. However, ENB is immunologically cold with low tumor mutational burden in most cases. Response rates likely lower than in highly immunogenic tumors. Potential benefit primarily in PD-L1-positive subset.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple cancer types including head and neck squamous cell carcinoma. Widely available at oncology centers. Off-label use for ENB may be covered under tumor-agnostic indications (TMB-high, MSI-high) if applicable.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized immune-related adverse event profile: thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, skin reactions. Generally manageable with corticosteroids. Better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy for most patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 expression variable (0-40%) in ENB. Low TMB limits immunotherapy responsiveness. MSI-high or TMB-high tumors (rare in ENB) would be best candidates. Genomic profiling recommended to identify potential responders.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Monoclonal antibodies blocking the PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing interaction with PD-L1 on tumor cells. This releases the immune checkpoint brake, allowing cytotoxic T cells to recognize and kill tumor cells. May be particularly effective in tumors with high PD-L1 expression, high tumor mutational burden, or mismatch repair deficiency.

key_evidence.study_name: Immune checkpoint inhibitors in refractory ONB: case reports

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for multiple indications; off-label for ENB

treatments.name: Everolimus (mTOR Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports of stable disease for approximately 12 months in ENB. Approved for other neuroendocrine tumors (pancreatic NETs) based on RADIANT-3 trial. PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway alterations found in subset of ENB. No prospective ENB-specific trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: Stable disease for approximately 12 months in case reports. Modest benefit primarily in slowing progression rather than tumor regression. Extrapolation from neuroendocrine tumor data (RADIANT-3: median PFS 11 months vs 4.6 months placebo).

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for pancreatic NETs, breast cancer, renal cell carcinoma, and tuberous sclerosis. Oral medication. Off-label for ENB potentially covered under neuroendocrine tumor indication.

safety_profile.rationale: Stomatitis (common), pneumonitis, hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, infections. Requires close monitoring. Better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy but significant side effect burden for a targeted agent.

biomarker_match.rationale: PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway alterations found in ~7% of ENB. mTOR pathway activation may be more common based on transcriptomic data. Best candidates identified through molecular profiling showing PIK3CA or PTEN alterations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Oral inhibitor of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), a serine/threonine kinase that integrates growth factor signaling, nutrient sensing, and cell proliferation. Blocks mTORC1 complex, inhibiting translation of cell cycle proteins and angiogenic factors. In neuroendocrine tumors, mTOR pathway activation drives proliferation and the VEGF-mediated angiogenic switch.

key_evidence.study_name: Case reports of everolimus in olfactory neuroblastoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for neuroendocrine tumors; off-label for ENB

clinical_trials.title: Natural History Study of Children and Adults With Olfactory Neuroblastoma
sites: NIH Clinical Center, Bethesda, MD, USA
clinical_trials.early_results: Ongoing natural history study collecting longitudinal data on disease course, treatment patterns, and molecular characterization. No treatment results yet available.

clinical_trials.title: Study of Nivolumab in Rare Tumors Including Olfactory Neuroblastoma (NCI-DART/SWOG S1609)
sites: Multiple US sites via SWOG network
clinical_trials.early_results: Basket trial evaluating nivolumab (anti-PD-1) plus ipilimumab in rare tumors. Neuroendocrine tumor cohort included. Preliminary data showed variable responses across rare tumor types.

clinical_trials.title: Lutetium-177 DOTATATE in SSTR2-Positive Rare Neuroendocrine Tumors
sites: European Neuroendocrine Tumor Society Centers of Excellence
clinical_trials.early_results: Three metastatic ENB cases treated with SSTR2-targeted PRRT were well-tolerated and resulted in stable disease. Larger cohort data pending.

clinical_trials.title: APEC1621 Pediatric MATCH: Targeted Therapy for Relapsed/Refractory Solid Tumors (includes ENB)
sites: Multiple US pediatric oncology sites via Children's Oncology Group
clinical_trials.early_results: Umbrella trial matching pediatric solid tumors to targeted therapies based on molecular profiling. Multiple treatment arms including FGFR, mTOR, and other pathway inhibitors.

clinical_trials.title: Proton Therapy for Sinonasal and Skull Base Malignancies Including ENB
sites: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX
Tsyb Medical Radiological Research Center, Obninsk, Russia
clinical_trials.early_results: Multiple institutional protocols evaluating proton and pencil beam scanning for skull base ENB. 2-year locoregional control 88.9% and OS 84.4% in 14-patient series. Ongoing accrual to establish optimal dose and fractionation.

clinical_trials.title: Molecular Profiling-Guided Targeted Therapy for Rare Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Basket trial approach for rare tumors with actionable genomic alterations. FGFR3 amplification (28% of ENB) and PIK3CA mutations (7%) are potentially matchable targets.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Adjuvant IMRT or Proton Beam Therapy (60-70 Gy)
combination_strategies.rationale: Surgery achieves gross total resection while adjuvant radiation eliminates microscopic residual disease. 5-year OS improves from 50.6% (surgery alone) to 84.4% (surgery + RT). Local control improves from 51.2% (RT alone) to 87.4% (surgery + RT). This is the backbone of ENB treatment for Kadish C.

Neoadjuvant platinum-etoposide downstages bulky Kadish C tumors with intracranial extension, achieving 82% ORR and enabling more complete surgical resection. Sequential trimodality (chemo-surgery-RT) is the UVA and Gustave-Roussy approach for advanced ENB with 5-year OS 72%.

Combined VEGFR and EGFR blockade produced 65% tumor reduction within 5 days in recurrent ENB with confirmed EGFR, FGFR2, KDR, and RET mutations on whole exome sequencing. Dual pathway inhibition may overcome resistance to single-agent TKI therapy.

Sequential multimodal targeted approach: PRRT delivers internal radiation via SSTR2 targeting, sunitinib provides anti-angiogenic maintenance, and SBRT addresses focal progression. Case report showed 12-month disease control with manageable toxicity in Kadish D disease.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Elective Neck Irradiation (45-50 Gy to bilateral levels I-III)
combination_strategies.rationale: ENI reduces cervical nodal failure from 18% to 0% at 5 years. Particularly important for Hyams III/IV tumors with higher metastatic potential. Adds modest treatment volume to comprehensive radiation plan. Cervical metastases eventually develop in 20-25% without elective treatment.

supportive_care.evidence: Institutional series consistently show improved outcomes at high-volume centers with dedicated skull base surgery teams (otolaryngology/neurosurgery co-surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, neuroradiology)
supportive_care.benefit: Optimized surgical planning, reduced complications, coordinated multimodality therapy. Centers with >20 ENB cases show superior outcomes vs low-volume centers.

supportive_care.evidence: 39% of ENB recurrences occur >5 years after initial treatment. Mean time to recurrence 4.74 years (up to 121 months). Hyams III recurs faster (3.75 years) than low-grade (5.70 years). Late recurrences documented at 10-20 years post-treatment.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of recurrence enables salvage therapy with 5-year post-recurrence OS of 63%. Protocol includes serial MRI with contrast every 3-6 months for 5 years, then annually for at least 10-15 additional years. Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT for surveillance in SSTR2+ cases.

supportive_care.evidence: Anosmia is nearly universal after craniofacial resection or endoscopic skull base surgery for ENB. Chronic sinusitis, crusting, and nasal congestion common postoperatively. Saline irrigations and mucosal care improve quality of life.
supportive_care.benefit: Regular nasal care with saline irrigations reduces crusting and infection risk. Olfactory rehabilitation may provide partial recovery in some endoscopic cases with unilateral approach. Psychosocial support for anosmia impact on quality of life.

supportive_care.evidence: Radiation to the anterior skull base (60-70 Gy) affects frontal lobes, temporal lobes, and hippocampi. IMRT/proton therapy reduces but does not eliminate cognitive risk. Temporal lobe necrosis occurs in 1.9% of IMRT patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Baseline and serial neurocognitive testing identifies early cognitive decline. Cognitive rehabilitation, occupational therapy, and compensatory strategies preserve functional independence. Proton therapy referral may reduce long-term cognitive risk.

supportive_care.evidence: ENB is a rare tumor requiring complex treatment with significant functional impacts. Young patient age (45) and long expected survivorship period increase psychosocial burden. Facial disfigurement concerns, anosmia, and cancer-related anxiety.
supportive_care.benefit: Psychological screening and counseling improve coping and quality of life. Support groups for rare tumor patients reduce isolation. Survivorship care plans address long-term monitoring needs and late effects of treatment.

supportive_care.evidence: 68% of ENB harbor at least one somatic genomic alteration. 51% carry clinically relevant genomic alterations with therapeutic potential. Key targets include FGFR3 (28%), TP53 (17%), PIK3CA (7%), CTNNB1 (16%), IDH2 R172 (35% basal-like). SSTR2 expression in 82-99%.
supportive_care.benefit: Identifies actionable targets for salvage therapy if recurrence occurs (sunitinib, pazopanib, everolimus, PRRT). Guides clinical trial eligibility. Confirms SSTR2 status for potential PRRT. Prognostic stratification based on molecular subtype (neural vs basal).
